summary: Scottish Enlightenment philosopher and empiricist. Author of A Treatise of Human Nature, An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding, and Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion. Developed the copy principle, Hume's fork (relations of ideas vs. matters of fact), the problem of induction, the is-ought problem, the concept of constant conjunction, and skepticism about miracles. His moral philosophy characterizes reason as the 'slave of the passions.' Influenced Kant's 'dogmatic slumber,' David Lewis's Humean supervenience, and the Scottish Enlightenment alongside Adam Smith.

works.name: Epistemology: Copy Principle, Hume's Fork, and Causation
works.description: Hume's epistemology centers on the copy principle (all simple ideas are copies of simple impressions), Hume's fork (dividing all objects of inquiry into 'relations of ideas' like the Pythagorean theorem and 'matters of fact' like the sun rising tomorrow), and his theory of causation as 'constant conjunction' rather than necessary connection. The problem of induction argues that the uniformity of nature cannot be proven; Thomas Reid and Nelson Goodman (grue/bleen) responded. The 'missing shade of blue' is a famous counterexample to the copy principle. Mary Shepherd critiqued his theory of causation. Kant credited Hume with waking him from his 'dogmatic slumber.'

clues.clue: copy principle — all simple ideas are copies of simple impressions
examples: all our ideas are products of impressions in his 'copy principle'
all simple ideas are derived from simple impressions

clues.clue: Hume's fork — relations of ideas (a priori, like Pythagorean theorem) vs. matters of fact (a posteriori, like the sun rising)
examples: distinguished between impressions and ideas
distinguished 'Relations of Ideas' and 'Matters of Fact'

clues.clue: causation as 'constant conjunction' — not necessary connection; illustrated by billiard balls
examples: developed an early version of the regulatory theory of causation, which posited a 'constant conjunction'
discusses the collision of billiard balls to show that we only perceive cause and effect through 'custom or habit'

a man who has experienced all colors except for a 'missing shade of blue'

clues.clue: books containing neither abstract reasoning nor experimental reasoning should be 'committed to the flames'
examples: suggested that books containing neither 'abstract reasoning concerning quantity' nor 'experimental reasoning' could be committed to flames

works.name: Moral Philosophy: Is-Ought Problem and Sentimentalism
works.description: Hume's moral philosophy argues that moral distinctions come from sentiment, not reason. 'Reason is, and ought only to be, the slave of the passions.' The is-ought problem (Hume's guillotine) argues that normative claims cannot be derived from factual propositions — 'every system of morality' establishes its own God. John Searle's response considers promising $5 to derive ought from is. The 'sensible knave' exploits moral rules without being caught. Hume denounced 'monkish virtues' like celibacy and fasting. He distinguished artificial (socially constructed) from natural virtues. Simon Blackburn interprets Hume as a quasi-realist; Annette Baier called him the 'woman's moral theorist.'

clues.clue: is-ought problem / Hume's guillotine — cannot derive normative claims from factual propositions
examples: formulated the is-ought problem
showing that 'the distinction of vice and virtue is not founded merely on the relations of objects'

works.name: Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion
works.description: Hume's posthumously published dialogues feature Cleanthes (experimental theist defending the design argument), Philo (skeptic, usually taken as Hume's voice), and Demea. Philo refutes the design argument by proposing alternative analogies — the universe could be compared to vegetation, generation, or an 'infinitely large spider.' The work discusses the problem of evil (architect metaphor: house subjects residents to noise and darkness) and argues against a 'future state' of life.

clues.clue: debate between Cleanthes (design argument), Philo (skeptic), and Demea about God's attributes
examples: centers on a debate about God's attributes between Demea, Cleanthes, and Philo

clues.clue: refutes design argument with analogies: vegetation, generation, infinite spider
examples: put forth metaphors of 'vegetation' and 'generation' as alternatives
believing the world was created by an 'infinitely large spider'

clues.clue: problem of evil — architect whose house subjects residents to 'noise, confusion, fatigue, darkness'
examples: formulation of the problem of evil includes the metaphor of an architect

clues.clue: argument against miracles (chapter 'Of Miracles') — evidence for laws of nature always outweighs single supernatural events
examples: argues that our evidence for the laws of nature will always outweigh evidence for single supernatural events

works.name: Aesthetics and Essays
works.description: Hume's essay 'Of the Standard of Taste' argues that beauty is subjective but identifies the 'joint verdict' of 'true judges/critics' as the standard. It uses a Don Quixote story about wine tasting (leather vs. iron) to illustrate 'delicacy of imagination.' Other Four Dissertations include 'Of Tragedy' (the paradox of tragedy — we gain pleasure from well-written tragedy) and 'The Natural History of Religion.' Hume also identified the 'paradox of tragedy.'

clues.clue: 'Of the Standard of Taste' — beauty is subjective; 'joint verdict' of 'true judges'; Don Quixote wine story for 'delicacy of imagination'
examples: A David Hume essay identifies the 'standard' of this faculty as the 'joint verdict' of 'true critics'
recounts a story from Don Quixote in which two men argue about whether a cask of wine tastes like leather or iron

clues.clue: Four Dissertations include 'Of Tragedy' and 'Of the Standard of Taste'
examples: this philosopher's Four Dissertations include 'Of Tragedy' and 'Of the Standard of Taste'

clues.clue: paradox of tragedy — we gain pleasure from watching well-written tragedy
examples: identified the 'paradox of tragedy,' whereby we gain pleasure from watching a well-written tragedy

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Scottish Enlightenment philosopher. Close friend of Adam Smith; Dennis Rasmussen's The Infidel and the Professor chronicles their friendship. Hume's Treatise 'fell still-born from the press.' James Beattie's Essay on Truth critiqued his racism in 'Of National Characters.' Jeremy Bentham's 'principle of utility' was inspired by Hume. Hume's essay on rowing a boat illustrates convention. He compared the mind to a theatre where perceptions 'pass, re-pass, glide away, and mingle.'

clues.clue: Scottish Enlightenment; friend of Adam Smith (The Infidel and the Professor); Treatise 'fell still-born from the press'
examples: Dennis Rassmussen's 2017 book The Infidel and the Professor
inspired the other to write a book that 'fell still-born from the press'

clues.clue: mind compared to a theatre — perceptions 'make their appearance; pass, re-pass, glide away, and mingle'
examples: compares the mind to one of these areas, where perceptions 'make their appearance; pass, re-pass, glide away, and mingle'

clues.clue: Beattie's Essay on Truth critiqued Hume's racism in 'Of National Characters'; Bentham's utility inspired by Hume
examples: James Beattie's Essay on Truth critiques the racism of this philosopher's essay 'Of National Characters'

clues.clue: Hume's principle — neo-Fregeans use it to define numbers; equinumerosity; 'Bad Company' problem
examples: A 'principle' that neo-Fregeans use to define numbers is named for this thinker

comprehensive_summary:
David Hume (1711-1776) is the most heavily clued philosopher of the British empiricist tradition, with an extraordinarily deep and varied question set spanning epistemology, ethics, philosophy of religion, and aesthetics.

Hume's epistemology centers on the copy principle (simple ideas copy simple impressions), Hume's fork (relations of ideas vs. matters of fact), and causation as 'constant conjunction' rather than necessary connection (illustrated by billiard balls). The 'missing shade of blue' is a counterexample to the copy principle. The problem of induction argues that the uniformity of nature cannot be proven — making inferences is 'custom or habit,' not reason. Nelson Goodman's grue/bleen extends this. Kant credited Hume with waking him from his 'dogmatic slumber.' David Lewis's 'Humean supervenience' holds the world is 'a vast mosaic of local matters of particular fact.'

Hume's moral philosophy argues that moral distinctions come from sentiment, not reason — 'reason is the slave of the passions.' The is-ought problem (Hume's guillotine) says normative claims cannot be derived from factual ones. He preferred the destruction of the whole world to pricking his finger, denounced 'monkish virtues,' and distinguished artificial from natural virtues. Simon Blackburn interprets him as a quasi-realist; Annette Baier called him the 'woman's moral theorist.'

Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion features Cleanthes (design argument), Philo (skeptic/Hume), and Demea. Philo refutes the design argument with alternative analogies (vegetation, generation, infinite spider). The 'Of Miracles' chapter argues evidence for natural law always outweighs supernatural claims.

Hume's aesthetics include 'Of the Standard of Taste' (beauty is subjective; the Don Quixote wine story illustrates 'delicacy of imagination') and the 'paradox of tragedy.' He was a close friend of Adam Smith and a figure of the Scottish Enlightenment.
